# Functions {.unnumbered} ## Python's batteries {.nostretch} ![](images/bateries-included.png){fig-align="center" width=55%}

**Python comes with "batteries included".**

The standard library provides built-in support for lots of common tasks, including the ability to interact with the operating system, open, read and write files, perform numerical & mathematical operations, and many more. ## Fundamentals Let's take a look at a simple example: ::: {.cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} print("Hello World") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Hello World ``` ::: ::: *Functions* take the form:

```function_name(arguments)```

where the ```arguments``` are the elements we want to apply the function to. Different functions require a different number and type of arguments. ::: {.callout-important} ### When using a function you must always use parentheses, even if they’re empty, so that Python knows a function is being called. ::: ## Common examples In addition to ```print```, there are other built-in functions that behave intuitively. Measuring the length of something: ::: {.cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} len('ABCDEFG') ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ``` 7 ``` ::: ::: Finding the minimum of a list of values: ::: {.cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} min(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` 1 ``` ::: ::: Finding the maximum of a list of values: ::: {.cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} max(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` 5 ``` ::: ::: :::{.callout-note} ### Every function takes a different number of arguments, and every function returns *something*. ::: ## Default arguments Functions may have default values for some arguments. For example, ```round``` will round off a floating-point number: ::: {.cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} round(3.3333333333, 4) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ``` 3.3333 ``` ::: ::: By default, it will round to zero decimal places if the second argument (the number of decimal places) is not provided. ::: {.cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} round(3.3333333333) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ``` 3 ``` ::: ::: :::{.callout-important} ### The position of the arguments here determines what is the number to be rounded, and what is the number of places to be rounded to. This is called providing *positional arguments*, and can lead to some hard-to-track bugs! ::: ## Help! ::: {.callout-tip} ### How do you find out what arguments, and in what order, different built-in functions require? ::: Of course, you could always consult the [official documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html) There is also a built-in ```help``` function that gives access to this info in your editor! ::: {.cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} help(max) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Help on built-in function max in module builtins: max(...) max(iterable, *[, default=obj, key=func]) -> value max(arg1, arg2, *args, *[, key=func]) -> value With a single iterable argument, return its biggest item. The default keyword-only argument specifies an object to return if the provided iterable is empty. With two or more arguments, return the largest argument. ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note} ### In jupyter, you can also add a ```?``` after a function name. ::: ## In practice ::: {.callout-caution} ### Exercises 1.1 Printing fundamentals 1.2 Using ```min()``` and ```max()``` to assess values 1.3 Explore the effect of default arguments and improperly formatted function calls :::
